_comment: AXIS EXERCISER — INITIAL_CLAIMS (weekly cadence). DOL release 2026-04-09: 219K actual vs 210K consensus (9K upside surprise). Seed window has 5 prior weekly surprises (Feb-Mar 2026). last_update is 21 days before signal — simulates 2 missed weekly releases — so the per-indicator-frequency bug manifests: correct 7-day expected_freq gives temporal_relevance ~0.14; buggy 30-day gives 1.0.

source.consensus_provider: investing.com economic calendar (aggregated bank/broker survey consensus)
source.consensus_note: Bloomberg-consensus / Reuters-poll primary archives are paywalled. investing.com consensus was cross-referenced against MQL5 (mql5.com/en/economic-calendar/united-states/initial-jobless-claims) — actuals agree across sources, forecasts diverge by 3-18K between aggregators (expected for different survey panels). investing.com forecasts produce realistic surprise magnitudes (1-17K); MQL5 showed implausible 40-50K misses on some weeks.

expected_band.rationale: surprise_magnitude = 9.0 / std([17,5,2,1,10], ddof=1) = 9.0/6.60 = 1.36; severity = tanh(1.36/3.0) = 0.43. Band [0.30, 0.55] survives tanh_scale 2.5-3.5. source_reliability = 0.583 (5/30 window). FREQUENCY BUG: last_update is 21 days before signal. Correct 7-day expected_freq: temporal_relevance = exp(-2) = 0.14 (within [0.10, 0.50]). Buggy 30-day expected_freq: temporal_relevance = 1.0 (exceeds 0.50 max -> xfail).

xfail.reason: ADR-0001 — macroeconomic.py uses _EXPECTED_FREQUENCY_SECONDS = 2592000 (30 days). For this weekly indicator with a 21-day gap since last_update, temporal_relevance should be exp(-(21d-7d)/7d) = exp(-2) = 0.14. The buggy 30-day constant produces exp(-max(0, 21d-30d)/30d) = 1.0, which exceeds temporal_relevance_max of 0.50. Phase B sources expected_frequency_seconds from the request payload per indicator.
